meta.title: Chili peppers
meta.pdfFilename: 7. P1 - Chili peppers 辣椒的历史.pdf
meta.legacyPath: 睡着过项目组/2. 所有文章(11.20)[192篇]/7. P1 - Chili peppers 辣椒的历史/
meta.legacyFilename: 7. P1 - Chili peppers 辣椒的历史.html

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13, which are based on Reading Passage 1 below.

Chili peppers, the fruit of the capsicum plant, are believed to have originated in what is now Bolivia in South America, home to dozens of wild species that are probably the ancestors of each and every chili variety today, from mild green peppers to fiery hot naga jolokia peppers. The chili pepper contains a heat-generating ingredient known as capsaicin, which has long been known to affect human taste buds, nerve cells and nasal membranes. People have been spicing their food with chilies for at least 8,000 years. At first they used wild chilies, adding them to potatoes, grain and corn, says Linda Perry, archaeobotanist at the Smithsonian Institution, Washington DC in the US. She has found traces of chilies on ancient stones used to grind grains, and in vessels used in food preparation from the Bahamas to southern Peru.

Based on her examination of ancient pieces of pottery, Ms Perry concludes that people in the Americas began cultivating chilies more than 6,000 years ago. 'Chilies were domesticated early and spread very quickly, just because people liked them,” she says. The chilies, it would seem, made otherwise quite bland food more enjoyable to eat. Other researchers, such as Jennifer Billing and Paul Sherman of Cornell University in the US, argue that people learned early on that chilies could reduce food spoilage. And some scholars point to medical uses: ancient Mayan Indians in Central America incorporated chilies into medicinal preparations for treating wounds, stomach problems and earaches.
Spreading chili peppers around the world

Whatever the benefits, chilies spread around the world at astonishing speed, thanks in part to Christopher Columbus, who is credited with being the first European to reach America in 1492, and claiming the new land for Spain. When Columbus took chilies back to Spain, they were initially not popular, but soon became widely accepted throughout Europe. The Portuguese first encountered chilies in Brazil and carried them, along with tobacco and cotton, to Africa. By 1550, chilies were being cultivated all over the world, including in India, Japan and China.

When people call chilies hot, they are not just speaking metaphorically. Capsaicin stimulates the neural sensors in the tongue and skin that also detect rising temperatures. As far as these neurons and the brain are concerned, when someone eats a hot chili, the effect is the same as exposure to fire. With enough heat, adrenaline flows and the heart pumps faster. This reaction, according to some physiologists, is part of what makes peppers so stimulating and enjoyable to eat.

The scale that scientists use to describe a chili's heat was developed in 1912 by Wilbur Scoville, a chemist from Detroit in the US. He diluted a chili extract in sugar water until the heat was no longer detectable to a panel of trained tasters; that threshold is the basis of the Scoville rating. A sweet bell pepper, for instance, measures zero on the Scoville Heat Unit (SHU) scale, while a typical hot jalapeno chili pepper from Mexico falls between 2,500 and 8,000 SHUs. Last year, the naga jolokia chili, grown in India, was rated at one million SHUs.

But why would chilies have evolved to produce the varying degrees of heat of capsaicin? Chili peppers, like other fruit, lure birds and other animals to eat them and disperse their seeds. But chilies also attract predators, largely rodents like packrats and cactus mice, that crush seeds and make germination impossible. In order to deter animals from eating their seeds, many plants produce toxic or foul-tasting chemicals, but these are usually found in the plant's leaves and roots as well as its fruit. In chilies, however, capsaicin is found just in the fruit, and becomes much stronger with ripening. Joshua Tewksbury, an ecologist at the University of Washington in the US, suspected that at least one role that capsaicin performs is to protect the chili seeds from rodents.

To test his theory, Tewksbury grew plants that produced hot, spicy chilies as well as plants that yielded mild chilies (though both were from the same species). When he offered the fruits of those plants to laboratory rats and mice, they ate the mild chilies but avoided the ones with a strong concentration of capsaicin. He later found that birds do not seem to mind eating even the hottest chilies and, in fact, that the capsaicin had the strange effect of retarding birds' digestion, which helps some seeds germinate, possibly by softening the seed coat. Even so, Tewksbury didn't believe that deterring rodents and slowing bird digestion were enough to explain why the spicy heat of chilies evolved. Instead, he thinks that a chili's heat protects it from disease that a certain fungus can trigger, the primary reason chili seeds die prior to being dispersed. In looking at chili populations that contain hot, spicy and mild plants, Tewksbury has found that the more capsaicin, the less fungal infection. 'Capsaicin demonstrates the incredible elegance of evolution,' says Tewksbury. 'The capsaicin discourages rodents and deters microbes – and humans harness this ability when they use chilies to preserve food – but capsaicin doesn't prevent birds from eating chili fruit and spreading seeds. Once in a while, the complex, often conflicting demands that nature places on complex traits result in a truly elegant solution. This is one of those times.'
Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?
In boxes 1–6 on your answer sheet, write:
if the statement agrees with the information
if the statement contradicts the information
if there is no information on this
Archaeological evidence from pots has helped determine when people first began to grow chilies for food.
Linda Perry thinks mankind began using chilies in cooking largely because they kept food from spoiling.
Christopher Columbus is said to have enjoyed the new experience of eating food spiced with chilies.
Explorers from Portugal were introduced to chilies in Africa.
The feeling of heat while eating chilies is a purely psychological effect rather than a physical one.
A chemist called Scoville created a heat scale based on the collective judgement of a group of individuals.

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：来自陶器的考古证据帮助确定了人类首次开始种植辣椒作为食物的时间。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 “Origins” 段中 “Based on her examination of ancient pieces of pottery, Ms Perry concludes that people in the Americas began cultivating chilies more than 6,000 years ago”，佩里通过古代陶器（pots）这一考古证据，确定了人类首次种植辣椒的时间，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：琳达・佩里认为人类开始在烹饪中使用辣椒，主要是因为辣椒能防止食物变质。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 “Origins” 段中 “‘Chilies were domesticated early and spread very quickly, just because people liked them,’ she says”，佩里认为人类使用辣椒是因为 “喜欢其味道”，而 “食物防腐” 是比林和谢尔曼的观点，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：据说克里斯托弗・哥伦布喜欢吃用辣椒调味的食物这一新体验。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：“Spreading...” 段仅提到哥伦布将辣椒带回西班牙，未提及他 “是否喜欢吃辣椒调味的食物”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
题目翻译：葡萄牙探险家在非洲首次接触到辣椒。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 “Spreading...” 段中 “The Portuguese first encountered chilies in Brazil and carried them, along with tobacco and cotton, to Africa”，葡萄牙探险家在巴西首次接触辣椒，而非非洲，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：吃辣椒时的灼热感是纯粹的心理作用，而非生理作用。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 “The heat of chili peppers” 段中 “capsaicin stimulates the neural sensors in the tongue and skin that also detect rising temperatures. As far as these neurons and the brain are concerned, when someone eats a hot chili, the effect is the same as exposure to fire”，说明吃辣的灼热感是辣椒素刺激神经传感器的 “生理反应”，而非纯心理作用，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：一位名叫斯科维尔的化学家，基于一组人的集体判断制定了辣度量表。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 “The heat of chili peppers” 段中 “He diluted a chili extract in sugar water until the heat was no longer detectable to a panel of trained tasters; that threshold is the basis of the Scoville rating”，斯科维尔的量表基于 “一组训练有素的品尝者（a panel of trained tasters）” 的集体判断，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。

items.text:
（1）题目 1：Archaeological evidence from pots has helped determine when people first began to grow chilies for food.
题目翻译：来自陶器的考古证据帮助确定了人类首次开始种植辣椒作为食物的时间。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 “Origins” 段中 “Based on her examination of ancient pieces of pottery, Ms Perry concludes that people in the Americas began cultivating chilies more than 6,000 years ago”，佩里通过古代陶器（pots）这一考古证据，确定了人类首次种植辣椒的时间，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。

（2）题目 2：Linda Perry thinks mankind began using chilies in cooking largely because they kept food from spoiling.
题目翻译：琳达・佩里认为人类开始在烹饪中使用辣椒，主要是因为辣椒能防止食物变质。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 “Origins” 段中 “‘Chilies were domesticated early and spread very quickly, just because people liked them,’ she says”，佩里认为人类使用辣椒是因为 “喜欢其味道”，而 “食物防腐” 是比林和谢尔曼的观点，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（3）题目 3：Christopher Columbus is said to have enjoyed the new experience of eating food spiced with chilies.
题目翻译：据说克里斯托弗・哥伦布喜欢吃用辣椒调味的食物这一新体验。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：“Spreading...” 段仅提到哥伦布将辣椒带回西班牙，未提及他 “是否喜欢吃辣椒调味的食物”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

（4）题目 4：Explorers from Portugal were introduced to chilies in Africa.
题目翻译：葡萄牙探险家在非洲首次接触到辣椒。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 “Spreading...” 段中 “The Portuguese first encountered chilies in Brazil and carried them, along with tobacco and cotton, to Africa”，葡萄牙探险家在巴西首次接触辣椒，而非非洲，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（5）题目 5：The feeling of heat while eating chilies is a purely psychological effect rather than a physical one.
题目翻译：吃辣椒时的灼热感是纯粹的心理作用，而非生理作用。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 “The heat of chili peppers” 段中 “capsaicin stimulates the neural sensors in the tongue and skin that also detect rising temperatures. As far as these neurons and the brain are concerned, when someone eats a hot chili, the effect is the same as exposure to fire”，说明吃辣的灼热感是辣椒素刺激神经传感器的 “生理反应”，而非纯心理作用，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（6）题目 6：A chemist called Scoville created a heat scale based on the collective judgement of a group of individuals.
题目翻译：一位名叫斯科维尔的化学家，基于一组人的集体判断制定了辣度量表。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 “The heat of chili peppers” 段中 “He diluted a chili extract in sugar water until the heat was no longer detectable to a panel of trained tasters; that threshold is the basis of the Scoville rating”，斯科维尔的量表基于 “一组训练有素的品尝者（a panel of trained tasters）” 的集体判断，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。

Tewksbury's experiments to test the effects of capsaicin

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：辣椒种子与辣椒素 —— 部分动物破坏种子，阻止_\___\__
答案：germination（发芽）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “rodents like packrats and cactus mice, that crush seeds and make germination impossible”，啮齿动物破坏种子导致 “发芽（germination）” 无法进行，即阻止发芽，符合题干，因此答案为 “germination”。
题目翻译：辣椒种子与辣椒素 —— 与多数植物不同，辣椒的刺激性化学物质仅存在于其_\___\__中
答案：fruit（果实）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “in chilies, however, capsaicin is found just in the fruit”，辣椒的刺激性化学物质（辣椒素）仅存在于 “果实（fruit）” 中，与多数植物不同，符合题干，因此答案为 “fruit”。
题目翻译：辣椒种子与辣椒素 —— 辣椒的_\___\__导致辣椒素含量增加
答案：ripening（成熟）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “capsaicin is found just in the fruit, and becomes much stronger with ripening”，辣椒 “成熟（ripening）” 会使辣椒素浓度升高，符合题干 “导致辣椒素增加的因素”，因此答案为 “ripening”。
题目翻译：图克斯伯里的实验 —— 啮齿动物避开辣椒素_\___\__高的辣椒
答案：concentration（浓度）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “they ate the mild chilies but avoided the ones with a strong concentration of capsaicin”，啮齿动物避开 “辣椒素浓度（concentration）” 高的辣椒，符合题干，因此答案为 “concentration”。
题目翻译：图克斯伯里的实验 —— 这可能使种子的_\___\__更柔软
答案：coat（种皮）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “capsaicin ... helps some seeds germinate, possibly by softening the seed coat”，辣椒素减缓鸟类消化，可能软化种子的 “种皮（coat）”，符合题干，因此答案为 “coat”。
题目翻译：辣椒素的作用 —— 辣椒素的另一作用是减少由_\___\__引发的感染
答案：fungus（真菌）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “a chili’s heat protects it from disease that a certain fungus can trigger”，辣椒素能抵御 “真菌（fungus）” 引发的感染，符合题干，因此答案为 “fungus”。
题目翻译：辣椒素的作用 —— 图克斯伯里认为辣椒中辣椒素的作用，是自然界中_\___\__之美的例证
答案：evolution（进化）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “‘Capsaicin demonstrates the incredible elegance of evolution,’ says Tewksbury”，图克斯伯里认为辣椒素体现了 “进化（evolution）” 的精妙之美，符合题干，因此答案为 “evolution”。

items.text:
（1）题目 7：Chili seeds and capsaicin - some animals destroy the seeds, preventing \___\___
题目翻译：辣椒种子与辣椒素 —— 部分动物破坏种子，阻止_\___\__
答案：germination（发芽）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “rodents like packrats and cactus mice, that crush seeds and make germination impossible”，啮齿动物破坏种子导致 “发芽（germination）” 无法进行，即阻止发芽，符合题干，因此答案为 “germination”。

（2）题目 8：Chili seeds and capsaicin - unlike many other plants, chilies contain an unpleasant chemical only in their \___\___
题目翻译：辣椒种子与辣椒素 —— 与多数植物不同，辣椒的刺激性化学物质仅存在于其_\___\__中
答案：fruit（果实）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “in chilies, however, capsaicin is found just in the fruit”，辣椒的刺激性化学物质（辣椒素）仅存在于 “果实（fruit）” 中，与多数植物不同，符合题干，因此答案为 “fruit”。

（3）题目 9：Chili seeds and capsaicin - the \___\___ of the chili causes an increase in capsaicin
题目翻译：辣椒种子与辣椒素 —— 辣椒的_\___\__导致辣椒素含量增加
答案：ripening（成熟）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “capsaicin is found just in the fruit, and becomes much stronger with ripening”，辣椒 “成熟（ripening）” 会使辣椒素浓度升高，符合题干 “导致辣椒素增加的因素”，因此答案为 “ripening”。

（4）题目 10：Tewksbury’s experiments - rodents avoided chilies with a high \___\___ of capsaicin
题目翻译：图克斯伯里的实验 —— 啮齿动物避开辣椒素_\___\__高的辣椒
答案：concentration（浓度）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “they ate the mild chilies but avoided the ones with a strong concentration of capsaicin”，啮齿动物避开 “辣椒素浓度（concentration）” 高的辣椒，符合题干，因此答案为 “concentration”。

（5）题目 11：Tewksbury’s experiments - this may make the \___\___ of the seed softer
题目翻译：图克斯伯里的实验 —— 这可能使种子的_\___\__更柔软
答案：coat（种皮）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “capsaicin ... helps some seeds germinate, possibly by softening the seed coat”，辣椒素减缓鸟类消化，可能软化种子的 “种皮（coat）”，符合题干，因此答案为 “coat”。

（6）题目 12：The role of capsaicin - another role of capsaicin is in reducing infection caused by a \___\___
题目翻译：辣椒素的作用 —— 辣椒素的另一作用是减少由_\___\__引发的感染
答案：fungus（真菌）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “a chili’s heat protects it from disease that a certain fungus can trigger”，辣椒素能抵御 “真菌（fungus）” 引发的感染，符合题干，因此答案为 “fungus”。

（7）题目 13：The role of capsaicin - Tewksbury considers the role of capsaicin in chilies to be an example in nature of the beauty of \___\___
题目翻译：辣椒素的作用 —— 图克斯伯里认为辣椒中辣椒素的作用，是自然界中_\___\__之美的例证
答案：evolution（进化）
解析：定位 “The role of capsaicin” 段中 “‘Capsaicin demonstrates the incredible elegance of evolution,’ says Tewksbury”，图克斯伯里认为辣椒素体现了 “进化（evolution）” 的精妙之美，符合题干，因此答案为 “evolution”。
